talk_title: Multiplexed functional assay of BRCA2 missense variants in HAP1 cells

targets.dependency_context: DNA repair viability assay in HAP1 haploid cells

models.name: HAP1
models.genotype_or_key_features: haploid human leukemia line, BRCA2 endogenous locus edited

MOA_summary: A multiplexed assay of variant effects (MAVE) was performed in haploid HAP1 cells where BRCA2 missense variants were introduced at the endogenous locus; cells were cultured and viability ratios (day 5 vs day 14) were sequenced to generate functional scores that distinguish loss‑of‑function (pathogenic strong) from benign variants, enabling calibration to ACMG classification and risk association.
key_findings: 502 missense variants (≈10‑11% of all missense) were classified as pathogenic strong, far exceeding the 30‑50 pathogenic missense in ClinVar.
Sensitivity and specificity of the BRCA2 MAVE against known standards were 93‑95% across multiple metrics.
Integration of functional scores with ACMG criteria reduced VUS from ~7,000 to 608 variants (≈90% reclassification).
Combined analysis of two independent BRCA2 MAVEs (HAP1 and mouse ESC) using an integrated VarCall model captured ~95% of variants and further reduced VUS to 396.
Case‑control analysis of pathogenic‑strong missense variants yielded an odds ratio of 4.5 for breast/ovarian cancer risk, comparable to truncating mutations (OR ~5.5).
Demonstrated that combining MAVEs improves variant classification accuracy and clinical utility.
open_questions: How do functional scores translate across different cell types (e.g., primary mammary epithelial cells vs HAP1)?
Can replication‑fork specific assays be integrated to capture BRCA2 functions beyond homologous recombination?

talk_title: Saturation MAVE of Lynch syndrome mismatch‑repair genes and clinical risk integration

models.name: haploid human cell line (e.g., HAP1)
models.genotype_or_key_features: knockout of each MMR gene, transduced with variant libraries

models.name: All of Us biobank cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: population‑scale genotype data linked to colorectal and endometrial cancer outcomes

MOA_summary: Saturation MAVE libraries covering >73,000 missense variants across the four Lynch‑syndrome mismatch‑repair genes were introduced into haploid cells; 6‑thioguanine treatment selects for loss‑of‑function variants, and deep sequencing quantifies variant depletion, producing functional scores that correlate with clinical pathogenicity and cancer risk.
key_findings: Generated functional maps for >73,000 missense variants across MSH2, MLH1, MSH6, and PMS2.
Proline substitution patterns validated assay performance, showing expected loss‑of‑function in ordered protein regions.
47 of 48 clinically classified missense variants (independent of functional evidence) were concordant with MAVE results, providing strong evidence for assay reliability.
Retrospective reclassification of 10 individuals in Ambry Genetics database as Lynch syndrome carriers based on MAVE data.
Population‑scale analysis linked loss‑of‑function missense scores to increased colorectal cancer risk, recapitulating known penetrance differences (high for MLH1/MSH2, lower for MSH6/PMS2).
AlphaMissense predictor overestimates pathogenicity (≈40% vs 12% loss‑of‑function by MAVE) highlighting need for calibration.
Pairwise double‑mutant screens revealed epistatic interactions where a mildly deleterious variant (e.g., K618A in MLH1) amplifies the functional impact of other missense changes.
open_questions: How to systematically capture and interpret phased double‑mutant haplotypes in clinical sequencing data?
What is the contribution of non‑viability phenotypes (e.g., replication‑fork stress) to pathogenicity that may be missed by the 6‑TG assay?
Can functional scores be integrated with polygenic risk scores to refine individual cancer risk predictions?
